note_data.title: Research Methods
keyPoints: Sociology is the study of human social life, groups and societies
Sociology explores the social factors that shape human behavior and the way that society influences our daily lives
Structuralism is a macro perspective where societies are seen as sets of structures in which individuals play different roles - individuals have little choice
Interpretivism is a micro level approach in which individual is important - people make the societies and meanings individuals give to society matter
Consensus approach focuses on equality in society or agreed norms, creating stable forms of society
Conflict approach involves societies where values are not shared such as societies with different wealth are unstable
Functionalism is a consensus approach that focuses on social functions and asks what keeps the society together
Marxism is a conflict approach focusing on different social classes - bourgeoisie (upper class) and proletariat (working class)
Feminism is a conflict approach where patriarchy exists and males are more dominant
Sociological research involves primary or secondary research types and sociologists must select and use evidence in a balanced way

definitions.definition: The study of human social life, groups and societies
Macro perspective where societies are seen as sets of structures in which individuals play different roles - individuals have little choice
Micro level approach in which individual is important - the meanings individuals give to the society

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed account of the method including what it involves and how it works
Give advantages AND disadvantages with detailed explanations

modelAnswer.question: Explain two advantages of using surveys in sociological research. [4 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: One advantage is that surveys can collect data from large numbers of people quickly and cheaply. This is because questionnaires can be distributed to hundreds of respondents simultaneously, making it cost-effective compared to individual interviews. A second advantage is that surveys produce quantitative data that can be easily compared and analyzed statistically. This is because closed questions with fixed response categories allow researchers to identify clear patterns and trends in the data.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Explanation of why this is an advantage
Mark 4: Explanation of statistical analysis benefit

commonMistakes: Not following proper procedures in research - others will question your research
Not selecting and using evidence in a balanced way - other sociologists may criticize the research
Confusing macro and micro perspectives in structuralism vs interpretivism
examinerTips: Important to follow proper procedures - others will question your research
Sociologists must select and use evidence in a balanced way or other sociologists may criticize the research
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Research Process Flowchart showing: Research Question → Hypothesis → Choose Method → Select Sample → Collect Data → Analyze Results → Draw Conclusions. Include feedback loops showing how conclusions can lead to new research questions.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q1 (15 marks) — they asked students to evaluate the view that quantitative research methods have more strengths than limitations
This topic was tested in O/N 2022 P2 Q2 (4 marks) — they asked students to describe two quantitative research methods
This topic was tested in M/J 2024 P2 Q3 (15 marks) — they asked students to evaluate the view that natural science methods should not be used in sociological research
This topic was tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to explain research methods and evidence to investigate migration patterns
This topic was tested in M/J 2022 P1 Source A (2 marks) — they asked students to identify two research methods used in a study

workedExample.question: Explain the research methods and evidence you would choose to investigate why people migrate from one country to another. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Choose two primary methods with clear sampling strategy (e.g., structured interviews with systematic sampling of recent migrants)
Step 2: Select one secondary source (e.g., government immigration statistics or UN migration reports)
Step 3: Explain practical reasons for each choice (interviews allow detailed exploration of personal motivations)
Step 4: Justify methods in context of migration research (statistics show patterns, interviews reveal individual experiences)
workedExample.answer: I would use structured interviews with a systematic sample of recent migrants to gather detailed data about their personal reasons for migration, such as economic opportunities or family reunification. This method allows for standardized comparison while capturing individual experiences. I would also use government immigration statistics as secondary data to identify migration patterns and trends over time, providing quantitative context. Additionally, I would conduct a postal questionnaire survey to reach a larger sample across different locations, using stratified sampling by country of origin to ensure representation of diverse migration experiences.
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for each primary method with sampling (4 marks total), 1 mark for secondary source, 3 marks for clear reasons and context linking methods to migration research

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to distinguish between structured and unstructured interviews' — always specify the type of interview and explain why that structure suits your research aim

Examiner Report 2024: 'Candidates often described methods without linking to sampling techniques' — always mention specific sampling methods (random, systematic, stratified) when discussing primary research

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently confused research methods with data types' — remember that questionnaires and interviews are methods, while quantitative and qualitative describe the type of data they produce
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw the Research Methods Classification Chart: A hierarchical diagram showing Primary Methods (top branch splitting into Quantitative: questionnaires, structured interviews, experiments; and Qualitative: unstructured interviews, participant observation, ethnography) and Secondary Methods (bottom branch splitting into Quantitative: official statistics, existing surveys; and Qualitative: personal documents, media analysis). Include sampling types (random, systematic, stratified, opportunity) as sub-branches under primary methods.
note_data.quickRecap: Sociology is the study of human social life, groups and societies, exploring social factors that shape human behavior. There are two main approaches: structuralism (macro perspective) and interpretivism (micro perspective). Research can follow consensus or conflict approaches, with major theories including functionalism, Marxism, and feminism. Proper research procedures and balanced use of evidence are essential.
